charts.title: Week 9 Topic Map
charts.description: High-level overview of all major topics in Week 9.

nodes.label:
Week 9
Mechanism Design & Auctions
nodes.note: Week 9 introduces mechanism design as reverse game theory: designing the rules so that equilibrium outcomes are socially desirable. Auctions are the central application, studied for efficiency, revenue, truthfulness, and robustness.

nodes.label:
Mechanism
Design
nodes.note: Mechanism design specifies action spaces, information flows, and payoff rules so that self-interested agents produce desired outcomes at equilibrium. It underpins auctions, voting systems, and resource allocation protocols.

nodes.label:
Single-Good
Auctions
nodes.note: Four classic formats: English (ascending open-cry), Dutch (descending open-cry), first-price sealed-bid, and Vickrey (second-price sealed-bid). English is strategically equivalent to Vickrey; Dutch to first-price.

nodes.label:
Bayesian Games
& BNE
nodes.note: Auctions are Bayesian games: each bidder has a private type (valuation) drawn from a common prior. A Bayes-Nash equilibrium is a strategy profile where each player maximises expected utility given beliefs about others' types.

nodes.label:
Vickrey Auction
& Truthfulness
nodes.note: In the second-price sealed-bid auction, bidding your true valuation is a weakly dominant strategy. The winner pays the second-highest bid, so overbidding and underbidding both weakly hurt. The auction is strategyproof.

nodes.label:
Revenue
Equivalence
nodes.note: Under i.i.d. continuous priors and risk neutrality, all four classic auction formats yield the same expected revenue to the seller. This theorem is a cornerstone of auction theory.

nodes.label:
Collusion &
Winner's Curse
nodes.note: Second-price/English auctions are vulnerable to bidder collusion (bidding rings). Dutch auctions resist collusion. Winner's curse: in common-value auctions, winning implies your estimate was the highest (probably an overestimate).

nodes.label: VCG Mechanism
nodes.note: VCG generalises Vickrey to multiple goods: allocate to maximise total welfare, then pay each agent the externality they impose on others. VCG is efficient, dominant-strategy truthful, individually rational, and weakly budget-balanced.

nodes.label:
Revelation
Principle
nodes.note: Any outcome implementable in BNE by some indirect mechanism can also be implemented by a direct, incentive-compatible mechanism where agents truthfully report their types. This justifies focusing on direct truthful mechanisms.

nodes.label:
Multi-Unit &
Combinatorial Auctions
nodes.note: Multi-unit auctions sell k identical goods; VCG reduces to the (k+1)-price auction. Combinatorial auctions sell multiple different goods and allow bidding on subsets, capturing complementarities between items.

nodes.label:
Double
Auctions
nodes.note: Double auctions have both buyers and sellers. Equilibrium is where demand meets supply. Continuous double auctions underlie financial markets.

charts.title: Vickrey Auction: Why Truthful Bidding is Dominant
charts.description: Decision-based proof that bidding true valuation theta is weakly dominant in a second-price sealed-bid auction.

nodes.label:
Bidder with valuation theta;
consider bidding b != theta
nodes.note: In a second-price auction, the winner pays the second-highest bid (not their own). We show that deviating from truthful bidding b=theta can never strictly help.

nodes.label:
Would bidding theta
cause you to WIN?
nodes.note: If your true value theta is above the second-highest bid, bidding truthfully wins. Consider whether deviating changes the outcome.

nodes.label:
Bidding lower (b < theta):
does it change who wins?
nodes.note: If you still win with b < theta (second price still below b), outcome unchanged. If you lose with b < theta (b falls below second price), you lose a positive-utility item. Bidding lower only hurts or is neutral.

nodes.label:
Bidding higher (b > theta):
now pay second price > theta?
nodes.note: If you won with theta, bidding higher changes nothing (you still win, pay the same second price). If you lost with theta, bidding higher might make you win — but then you pay the second-highest bid, which exceeds your valuation theta. Negative utility.

nodes.label:
No change:
truthful is weakly best
nodes.note: Deviating from theta when you would have won anyway makes no difference (you pay the same second price regardless of your bid, as long as you win).

nodes.label:
Deviation hurts:
you lose a positive-value item
nodes.note: By underbidding below the second price, you lose an item that would have given you positive utility (theta - second price > 0). Truthful bidding was better.

nodes.label:
Deviation hurts:
negative utility if you win
nodes.note: By overbidding, you may win an item at a price above your valuation. You pay more than the item is worth to you. Truthful bidding was better.

nodes.label:
Truthful bidding
is weakly dominant
nodes.note: In all cases, bidding your true valuation theta is at least as good as any other bid. Truthful bidding is a weakly dominant strategy: no bid can strictly improve your expected utility.

charts.title: VCG Mechanism: Allocation and Transfers
charts.description: Step-by-step computation of allocations and payments in the VCG mechanism.

nodes.label:
Start: n agents report
valuations theta-hat
nodes.note: Each agent i reports their valuation function theta-hat_i. Under dominant-strategy truthfulness, they report their true valuation. The mechanism uses these reports to compute allocation and payments.

nodes.label:
Compute efficient allocation:
maximise total reported welfare
nodes.note: Find the feasible allocation x* that maximises the sum of all agents' reported valuations. This is the allocation that would be chosen by a social planner who knew all valuations.

nodes.label:
For each agent i,
compute transfer t_i
nodes.note: The VCG transfer for agent i is the sum of other agents' welfare under x* minus the maximum total welfare of other agents without i. This equals the externality i imposes on others.

nodes.label:
Is transfer t_i
positive?
nodes.note: The transfer can be positive (mechanism pays agent i) or negative (agent i pays the mechanism). In most auction settings, winners pay (negative transfer) and losers pay nothing.

nodes.label:
Agent i pays |t_i|
to the mechanism
nodes.note: A negative transfer means agent i pays the mechanism an amount equal to the harm their presence causes to other agents (e.g., displacing a higher-valued user).

nodes.label:
Mechanism pays t_i
to agent i
nodes.note: A positive transfer is rare in standard auctions but can occur in settings where agent i's presence creates positive externalities for others.

nodes.label:
Agent i's utility:
v(x_i, theta_i) + t_i
nodes.note: Under quasi-linear utility, agent i's payoff is their value for the allocation plus their transfer. Truthful reporting maximises this under VCG regardless of others' reports.

nodes.label:
VCG outcome:
efficient, truthful, IR
nodes.note: The VCG mechanism is efficient (maximises total welfare), dominant-strategy truthful (no agent benefits from misreporting), individually rational (all agents get non-negative utility), and weakly budget-balanced (never runs a deficit).

charts.title: Auction Format Classification
charts.description: Taxonomy of auction formats by information revelation and payment rule.

nodes.label:
Auction Formats
Classification
nodes.note: Auction formats differ in how bids are communicated (open or sealed) and how the winner pays (own bid or second-highest bid). Strategic equivalences exist between pairs of formats.

nodes.label:
Open-Cry
(dynamic bidding)
nodes.note: Open-cry auctions reveal bids publicly in real time. Bidders can react to others' bids. Two types: ascending (English) and descending (Dutch).

nodes.label:
Sealed-Bid
(simultaneous)
nodes.note: Sealed-bid auctions collect all bids simultaneously without revealing them to others. Two types: first-price (pay own bid) and second-price (pay second-highest bid).

nodes.label:
English Auction:
ascending, winner pays own bid
nodes.note: Price rises until only one bidder remains. Strategically equivalent to Vickrey: dominant strategy is to stay until price equals your valuation. Both give same equilibrium outcome.

nodes.label:
Dutch Auction:
descending, winner pays current price
nodes.note: Price falls until a bidder accepts. Strategically equivalent to first-price sealed-bid: bidders face the same trade-off between winning probability and payment. Resistant to collusion.

nodes.label:
First-Price Sealed-Bid:
pay own bid, shade below value
nodes.note: Bidders submit sealed bids; highest wins and pays their own bid. BNE strategy is to shade bid to (n-1)/n times true value (for n bidders, uniform priors). Strategically equivalent to Dutch.

nodes.label:
Vickrey (Second-Price):
pay second-highest bid
nodes.note: Highest bidder wins but pays the second-highest bid. Truthful bidding is dominant. Strategically equivalent to English auction. Revenue equivalent to Dutch and first-price under standard assumptions.
